title: Share of deaths from suicide
subtitle: Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.
caption:
Share of deaths from suicide

Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.

The line chart tracks the estimated share of deaths from suicide for seven countries from 1980 to 2023, with the vertical axis ranging from 0% to over 6%. South Korea exhibits the most significant fluctuation, starting near 2.3% in 1980 and rising steadily to a peak above 6% around 2010. Following this maximum, the South Korean line descends sharply, dropping below 4% by 2023 before a slight uptick at the end of the period. Japan begins the timeline with the highest values, hovering between 3% and 3.5% until the early 1990s, after which it trends downward to finish between 1.5% and 2%. The United States and Germany maintain relatively stable trajectories in the lower-middle range; the United States line stays mostly between 1.5% and 1.8%, while Germany fluctuates between 1.2% and 1.6%. The United Kingdom remains consistently near the 1% mark throughout the forty-year span. Peru and Greece occupy the lowest bands, generally staying below 1%. Peru shows a gradual increase from roughly 0.2% to nearly 0.8% by 2023, while Greece remains flat near 0.4% for most of the period before dipping slightly. The data reflects modeled estimates intended to adjust for underreporting and missing data across these nations.
